1. Executive Summary

Quick assessment of all 22 scene(s).  ≥ 80 GOOD  60–79 WATCH  40–59 WARN  < 40 CRITICAL.  Full table: §2.

  • 1_260111, 6_260128, 8_260213, 11_260222, 14_260303, 15_260305, 16_260212, 17_260213, 19_260215 — GOOD (≥ 80): stable radiometric and geometric quality.
  • 2_260114, 3_260117, 4_260120, 5_260123, 10_260220, 12_260227, 20_260216, 21_260221, 22_260227 — WATCH (60–79): 2_260114 → Coreg shift; 3_260117 → Noise floor; 4_260120 → Coreg shift; 5_260123 → Coreg shift; 10_260220 → Striping; 12_260227 → Coreg shift; 20_260216 → Hot pixels; 21_260221 → Noise floor; 22_260227 → Coreg shift.
  • 7_260129, 9_260219, 13_260227, 18_260214 — WARN/CRITICAL (< 60): 7_260129 → Coreg shift; 9_260219 → Coreg shift; 13_260227 → Coreg shift; 18_260214 → Coreg shift.
  • Low coreg reliability: 3_260117, 4_260120, 7_260129, 13_260227, 18_260214 — fewer than 30 % of bands are high-confidence.

Column abbreviations:
Sat.=saturation  ·  Dead=dead_pixels  ·  Hot=hot_pixels  ·  Stripe=striping  ·  Co-Registration=coreg  ·  Noise=noise_floor  ·  DR=dynamic_range  ·  spectral_rough=spectral_rough

👁 Usability = weighted mean (§5.2): Stripe×2, Coreg×2, Noise×1, SpectralRough×1, DR×0.5, (Dead+Hot)/2×0.5.
Total = arithmetic mean of sub-scores (§5.1). Cap: striping sub < 40 → total ≤ 70 (annotated ↓ cap).
≥ 80 GOOD  60–79 WATCH  40–59 WARN  < 40 CRITICAL

B · Definitions

3. Scoring Framework Overview

The assessment is organised around three quality dimensions:

  • Sensor Health — Saturation, dead/hot pixels, calibration anomalies. Indicators of instrument health independent of scene content.
  • Radiometric Quality — Noise floor, dynamic range, striping, spectral consistency. Governs the fidelity of retrieved atmospheric signals.
  • Geometric Quality — Coregistration accuracy across spectral bands. Determines spatial alignment for multi-band index computation.

Score mapping

Each metric produces a sub-score Si ∈ [10, 100] via a piecewise-linear function between Tgood (→ 100) and Tbad (→ 10). Thresholds are either archive-derived (relative to observed instrument behaviour) or fixed guardrails (physically motivated). Full definitions: §4.

Reliability-aware adjustments

When evidence supporting a score is sparse, a soft coverage multiplier reduces it. For coregistration: if fewer than 20% of bands are high-confidence, the score is scaled toward ×0.5. The raw hc% is shown as a dedicated column in the table so the reader can judge confidence directly.

Composite score and caps

The total score is the arithmetic mean of all sub-scores. A hard cap prevents deceptively high totals: if the striping sub-score is below 40 the total is capped at 70. Capped scenes are annotated ↓ cap. Full formula: §5.

4. Formal Metric Definitions

Each subsection is collapsible — click the title to expand. All metrics use the common piecewise-linear score mapping below.

Common piecewise-linear score mapping (lower-is-better convention): S(x; T_good, T_bad) = 100 if x ≤ T_good 10 + 90 × (T_bad − x) / (T_bad − T_good) if T_good < x < T_bad 10 if x ≥ T_bad For higher-is-better metrics (DR util, inter-band corr): reverse convention, x ≥ T_good → 100, x ≤ T_bad → 10.
4.1 Saturation

Definition

Fraction of pixels with DN ≥ 1023 (10-bit saturation level), per band.

sat_frac = Σ(DN ≥ 1023) / (rows × cols) [per band, then worst-band drives section]

Thresholds (fixed guardrails)

Levelsat_frac
PASS< 0.0100%
WATCH< 0.100%
WARN< 1.00%
CRITICAL≥ 1.00%

Rationale

Saturation clips spectral information irrecoverably. Even modest saturation (> 0.1%) indicates integration-time or gain settings mismatched to scene brightness. Short-wave bands most relevant to aerosol retrieval are typically the first to saturate over bright targets.

4.2 Dead / Hot Pixels

Definition — Dead pixels (persistence-based two-pass)

zero_count[r,c] = number of bands with DN[r,c] = 0 Structural nodata : zero_count ≥ ceil(N × 95%) → excluded HC dead (scoring): zero_count ≥ ceil(N × 80%) → dead_hc_frac Suspected (diag.) : zero_count ≥ ceil(N × 30%) → dead_susp_frac dead_hc_frac = Σ(HC dead pixels) / (rows × cols) [structural nodata excluded]

Definition — Hot pixels

hot_frac = Σ( |DN − uniform_filter5(DN)| > 5 × σ_noise ) / (rows × cols)

Thresholds (fixed guardrails)

Leveldead_hc_frachot_frac
PASS< 0.100%< 0.0100%
WATCH< 1.00%< 0.0500%
WARN< 5.0%< 0.100%
CRITICAL≥ 5.0%≥ 0.100%

Rationale

The persistence test avoids false positives from naturally dark scene features (water, shadow) that are zero only in a minority of bands. Requiring DN = 0 in ≥ 80% of bands isolates detector-level failures. Structural nodata (coregistration fill wedges, zero in ≥ 95% of bands) is excluded to avoid inflating counts near image borders.

4.3 Noise Floor

Definition

σ_noise = std( DN − uniform_filter(DN, 5×5) ) evaluated on the bottom 15% of pixels by local gradient magnitude (flat, homogeneous scene regions only)

Archive-derived thresholds

T_good = P25(σ_noise across baseline pool) → score 100 T_bad = P75(σ_noise across baseline pool) → score 10 Fallback guardrails: PASS < 3.0 DN · WATCH < 5.0 DN · WARN ≥ 8.0 DN

See §6 for baseline pool definition.

Reliability signal

The homogeneous-pixel fraction (noise_homog_frac) is shown as a diagnostic line inside the Noise cell (e.g. 9.71 DN | 15% Homog%). Values below 5% are highlighted amber — the noise estimate rested on very few pixels and should be treated with caution.

Rationale

The 5×5 residual on flat regions isolates electronic read noise and dark current from scene texture. Archive normalisation adapts to the actual operating noise level of this instrument rather than a design specification.

4.4 Dynamic Range Utilization

Definition

DR_util[b] = ( P99(DN[b]) − P1(DN[b]) ) / 1023 DR_util = median( DR_util[b] ) over all bands

Score mapping (guardrail, higher-is-better)

S_DR(x) = 100 if x ≥ 5% 10 + 90 × x / 5% if x < 5% (0% → 10, 5% → 100)

Only sub-5% utilization is penalized. Above this floor the score is always 100 — scene brightness variation is irrelevant to instrument health.

Rationale

A 5% floor catches clear integration-time / gain misconfiguration while ignoring normal spectral variation across bands. Smoke plumes typically provide strong contrast, so low DR rarely limits smoke detection specifically.

4.5 Striping

Definition

col_mean[c] = mean DN of column c (per band) Δcol[c] = col_mean[c] − col_mean[c−1] (first difference) stripe_ratio = σ(Δcol) / σ(col_mean) Stripe coherence (Pearson r between col_mean profiles in top vs bottom half): r ≤ 0.30 → scene structure, no coherence penalty r ≥ 0.80 → consistent detector column bias (penalty applied)

Thresholds (fixed guardrails)

Levelstripe_ratio
PASS< 0.08
WATCH< 0.15
WARN< 0.30
CRITICAL≥ 0.30

Score cap

If the stripe sub-score < 40, the total scene score is capped at 70 regardless of other metrics. See §5.1.

Rationale

Striping corrupts column-mean spectra that retrieval algorithms rely on — one of the most damaging artifacts for hyperspectral analysis. The coherence test distinguishes instrument-driven column bias (dangerous) from scene-driven apparent striping (acceptable), preventing false CRITICAL flags over urban grids or coastlines. The high weight in the Usability Index reflects this centrality to smoke detection.

4.6 Coregistration Quality

Definition

spread[b] = √( Var([top_y, mid_y, bot_y]) + Var([top_x, mid_x, bot_x]) ) HC band : spread[b] ≤ 4 px hc_frac = |HC bands| / |all bands| W10 = mean of top-10% of |shift| magnitudes in the HC band set (worst-decile mean: more robust than single-band max)

Archive-derived thresholds

T_good = P75( W10 in baseline pool ) → shift score 100 T_bad = max( P95( W10 in baseline pool ), T_good + 5 px ) → shift score 10 Fallback (archive < 3 entries): T_good = 3.0 px, T_bad = 12.0 px

Score mapping with coverage multiplier

cov_mult = 0.5 + 0.5 × clamp( (hc_frac − 0.2) / (1 − 0.2), 0, 1 ) score_shift = S(W10; T_good, T_bad) [standard piecewise] coreg_sub = round(score_shift × cov_mult, 1) hc_frac = 20% → cov_mult = 0.50 (maximum soft penalty) hc_frac = 100% → cov_mult = 1.00 (no penalty)

Rationale

W10 (worst-decile mean) is more robust than the single worst band and more informative than the mean — it captures systematic bad-band clusters. Archive normalisation makes scores relative to what this sensor actually achieves. The coverage multiplier prevents a scene where most bands are low-confidence from appearing well-registered on the strength of a few easy bands. The raw hc% column allows direct inspection of coregistration reliability.

4.7 Spectral Consistency

Definition

spectral_rough = mean( |mean_DN[b+1] − mean_DN[b]| ) for b = 1 … N−1 Inter-band correlation (diagnostic, not scored here): corr[b] = Pearson r( col_mean_profile[b], col_mean_profile[b+1] )

Thresholds (fixed guardrails)

Levelspectral_rough (DN)inter-band corr
PASS< 5> 0.95
WATCH< 10> 0.90
WARN/CRIT≥ 20≤ 0.80

Rationale

A smooth spectral response is required for atmospheric correction and spectral unmixing. Large band-to-band jumps indicate calibration errors or bad bands. The metric is scene-independent — both forest and desert should yield smooth spectral profiles after calibration.

4.8 Calibration Coefficient Audit

Definition

gain_anom[b] : |gain[b] − moving_median(gain, 11-band window)| > 7 × MAD(gain) offset_anom[b] : |offset_dn[b] − median(offset_dn)| > 7 × MAD(offset_dn)

Rationale

No absolute thresholds are applied — the audit is self-referential, flagging bands that deviate from the instrument's own calibration trend. This catches band-specific calibration failures (bad pixel in calibration source, dead detector) without being confused by the natural spectral shape of the instrument response. Full per-band table: §7.1.

5. Composite Scores

5.1 Total Score

S_total = (1/M) × Σ S_i (M = number of active metrics, each S_i ∈ [10, 100])

Score cap rule:

if S_striping < 40: S_total = min(S_total, 70) Prevents a scene with catastrophic striping from scoring deceptively high via strong performance elsewhere.

5.2 Smoke Detection Usability Index

U = Σ( w_k × S_k ) / Σ( w_k ) Weights: Stripe = 2.0, Coreg = 2.0, Noise = 1.0, SpectralRough = 1.0 DR = 0.5, (Dead + Hot)/2 = 0.5 → denominator = 7.0 Dead and Hot are averaged first, then treated as a single 0.5-weight component.

Weight rationale:

  • Stripe × 2: column striping corrupts spectral signatures across the full swath — the most damaging artifact for smoke retrievals.
  • Coreg × 2: multi-band aerosol indices require accurate per-pixel band alignment; even 2 px shift degrades ratio accuracy.
  • Noise × 1 / SpectralRough × 1: moderate impact; poor SNR or rough spectra degrade retrievals but can sometimes be mitigated in post-processing.
  • DR × 0.5: under-utilisation is rarely a smoke-detection issue; smoke provides strong spectral contrast.
  • (Dead+Hot)/2 × 0.5: localised artifacts affect only a small pixel fraction; spatially-aware retrieval can mask them.
C · Reference

6. Archive Baseline Determination

Two metrics use archive-derived thresholds: Coregistration (W10) and Noise Floor (σ). Both follow the same P75/P95 framework.

Baseline pool

Baseline archive
1256
N = 4 scene(s) ·  override with --baseline

Only these scenes contribute to threshold estimation. Scenes with clear instrumentation anomalies should be excluded from the baseline pool.

Coregistration thresholds

T_good = P75( W10_hc in baseline pool ) T_bad = max( P95( W10_hc in baseline pool ), T_good + 5 px ) The +5 px floor ensures T_bad > T_good even with a small archive. Fallback when archive < 3 scenes: T_good = 3.0 px, T_bad = 12.0 px.

Coreg W10 scoring thresholds (baseline IDs = [1, 2, 5, 6], N = 4): Tgood = 3.00 px, Tbad = 12.00 px (max(baseline), floored at Tgood + 5 px).
archive P75 = 14.9 px, P95 = 15.2 px → absolute caps applied (Tgood ≤ 3.0 px, Tbad ≤ 12.0 px).
Baseline W10 values: 1_260111 = 14.71 px, 2_260114 = 12.47 px, 5_260123 = 15.17 px, 6_260128 = 14.81 px.

Noise thresholds

P25 / P75 of the baseline noise floor distribution → T_good / T_bad. As the archive grows the thresholds stabilise and scores become more reliable. Recording the baseline IDs and computed thresholds (see §8) ensures reports are reproducible.

Archive growth

Adding more baseline scenes may shift T_good / T_bad slightly. Sub-scores will update accordingly on the next full re-run. Stable baselines are expected after ≥ 6 scenes; with fewer entries the fallback values remain active for noisy estimates.

7. Diagnostic Appendices

Full per-scene diagnostic plots and raw metrics (noise DN, max coreg shift, DR utilisation, dead-pixel persistence maps, figures 00–16) are in each scene's individual quality report. Links available in the Image column of §2.

7.1 Calibration Coefficient Audit

Per-band gain and offset_dn coefficients flagged by robust outlier detection relative to the instrument's own coefficient curves — no absolute thresholds applied.  Overall: WARN  Flagged bands: 13

Gain: |gain − moving-median(11-band)| > 7×MAD.  offset_dn: |offset_dn − median| > 7×MAD. Method: §4.8.

BandGainOffsetOffset (DN)Reason flagged
B11.3680-1.5-1.1gain_outlier(29.8σ, smooth=0.6980)
B220.8630-4.4-5.1gain_outlier(7.8σ, smooth=0.6870)
B420.303064.5212.7offset_dn_outlier(24.7σ, val=212.7)
B430.323065.5202.7offset_dn_outlier(23.6σ, val=202.7)
B440.352068.4194.2offset_dn_outlier(22.6σ, val=194.2)
B750.323057.5178.0offset_dn_outlier(20.7σ, val=178.0)
B760.326041.2126.5offset_dn_outlier(14.7σ, val=126.5)
B810.5960173.8291.6gain_outlier(12.0σ, smooth=0.3250), offset_dn_outlier(33.9σ, val=291.6)
B820.6820-43.0-63.1gain_outlier(15.9σ, smooth=0.3250), offset_dn_outlier(7.3σ, val=-63.1)
B1430.330020.863.1offset_dn_outlier(7.4σ, val=63.1)
B1440.323021.065.0offset_dn_outlier(7.6σ, val=65.0)
B1460.4730-30.0-63.5offset_dn_outlier(7.3σ, val=-63.5)
B1490.254027.5108.2offset_dn_outlier(12.6σ, val=108.2)

Detection method: robust outlier detection on the instrument's own coefficient curves. Gain: |gain − moving-median smooth| > 7×MAD (window = 11 bands). Offset DN: |offset_dn − median| > 7×MAD. No absolute gain/offset thresholds are applied.

8. Implementation Notes

Key constants active at report-generation time. Any change to these values requires re-running quality_assessment.py --all to propagate updated scores.

ConstantValue
DN_SAT_THRESH1023
DEAD HC / SUSP / NODATA80% / 30% / 95%
STRIPE PASS / WATCH / WARN0.08 / 0.15 / 0.3
NOISE PASS / WATCH / WARN3.0 / 5.0 / 8.0 DN
DR_WARN (penalty floor)5%
COREG_CONF_SPREAD_PX4 px
COREG_COVERAGE_MIN20%
COREG FALLBACK T_good / bad3.0 / 12.0 px
Score cap rulestriping sub < 40 → total ≤ 70
BASELINE_IMAGE_IDS1 2 5 6
Usability weightsStripe×2, Coreg×2, Noise×1, Spectral×1, DR×0.5, DeadHot×0.5
